# Aletheia's Quest — guide for AI agents helping participants

You are an AI agent (Claude) helping someone **compete** in *Aletheia's Quest*, a
deception-detection competition. This file is your map: the competition, the repo,
how to craft and submit a method, how NDIF/nnsight work, and how to diagnose the
errors people actually hit. Read it before answering, and prefer the patterns here
over guessing — the runtime (NDIF + the sandbox) has sharp edges this file records.

When a participant is stuck in a way this file doesn't cover, point them to the
**[nnsight tutorials](https://nnsight.net/tutorials/)**, **[nnsight.net](https://nnsight.net)**,
the **[Discord](https://discord.gg/MfjQbAErEG)**, or the **office hours** (Tues & Fri,
1 PM ET — see the README). Don't invent NDIF/nnsight behavior; verify or defer.

---

## 1. What the competition is

Build a **deception detector**. For each conversation in a held-out eval set, output
the probability that the assistant's **final message** is *deceptive*. You build the
detector with [nnsight](https://nnsight.net) and run the heavy model compute remotely
on [NDIF](https://ndif.us); you submit a notebook, the leaderboard runs it against
private data and scores it.

**To compete you must be an official participant.** Apply at
**[aletheias-quest.github.io](https://aletheias-quest.github.io/)**; the organizers
issue you an **NDIF API key** for the competition cluster (this key authenticates
your remote traces — it's also your leaderboard identity). Standings live at the
**[leaderboard](https://ndif-leaderboard-dev.hf.space/)**.

The competition HF org (datasets live here): **<https://huggingface.co/aletheias-quest>**.

### Competition facts (apply, prizes, dates, eligibility, rules)
Authoritative source: **<https://aletheias-quest.github.io/>** (details may change; defer to it).

- **The framing.** Detect when an LLM *lies* — "the assistant lies if it states something it
  believes to be false." You get **black-box and white-box** access to a suite of LLMs
  ("**model organisms**") induced to lie in different ways. Methods are categorized as
  **white-box** (use activations/weights) or **black-box** (query-only).
- **How to apply.** Application form: <https://forms.office.com/r/BXVwPEweJi> (rolling; rated on
  team + technical approach; NDIF API access granted on a needs basis). Contact:
  **competition@cadenzalabs.org** or the Discord.
- **Timeline (2026).** Apply opens **Jun 4**, closes **Jun 21**; competition **Jun 29 → Jul 26**
  (main deadline); **bonus week** (surprise task) **Jul 27 → Jul 31**; invite-only **awards Aug 25**
  in Boston.
- **Prizes: $50,000 total**, split evenly among a team's members; a team can place in multiple
  categories and **prizes stack**. Categories: **white-box** (1st/2nd/3rd), **black-box**
  (1st/2nd/3rd), **weekly leaderboard** (1st/2nd/3rd), **judge's awards** (Novelty, Scalability).
  **All require ranking above the 4 baseline methods.**
- **Scoring.** Primary = **mean balanced accuracy** across datasets; tie-break = **average AUROC**;
  computed per *(model organism, dataset)* unit, averaged across all datasets.
- **Eligibility.** 18+; not a US-sanctioned/restricted party; not an organizer or dataset builder;
  one team per person; winners pass Northeastern's Restricted Party Screening; government employees
  need an employer release before award.
- **Method rules (these constrain your approach).** *Allowed:* querying via NDIF/nnsight,
  read/write activations at any layer, using weights + LoRA adapters, **tuning detector
  *parameters* per organism (documented)**, prompts from a *general* hypothesis. *Prohibited:*
  exfiltrating test data; reading model design docs/training data; **fundamentally different
  detection *logic* per organism**; prompts crafted from organism-specific behavioral analysis;
  **external API calls at inference**; **fine-tuning the hosted models**; organism-specific tricks
  that don't generalize. (Per-organism *parameters* yes; per-organism *logic* no.)
- **What you submit.** One notebook (one detector per organism) + trained params/supplemental data,
  a **report** of your method + per-organism choices, **reproducible training code**, and a
  compliance form with your team number. ≤ **200 MB**; daily submissions may be rate-limited. All
  submissions published **MIT-licensed** (you keep authorship; organizers get a perpetual
  royalty-free license to publish).
- **Organizers/sponsors.** Cadenza Labs, NDIF (Northeastern), Schmidt Sciences. Leads: Walter
  Laurito & Kieron Kretschmar (Cadenza Labs), Jaden Fiotto-Kaufman (NDIF), Peter Hase (Schmidt
  Sciences); advisor David Bau (NDIF Director).

---

## 2. Repo map

| Path | What it is |
|---|---|
| `submission/` | **What you submit.** Put your one notebook here. `util.py` lives here too. |
| `submission/util.py` | The toolkit (Section 5). Ships with your submission; import it from your notebook. |
| `submission/requirements.txt` | Your extra pip deps, installed into the per-job venv before your notebook runs. |
| `submission/example.ipynb` | Minimal working baseline (random scores) — the template to copy. |
| `baselines/` | Reference methods (mean-difference probe, llm-judge, self-eval, …) with `.npz` weights. Copy one into `submission/` to use it. |
| `tutorials/` | Walkthroughs: setup, predicting, training a probe. Start here to learn nnsight + the data. |
| `dry.yaml` | Which datasets `python submit.py --dry` rehearses on (edit to test on different dev sets). |
| `submit.py` | Packages the repo and submits; `--dry` runs the full pipeline locally first. |
| `leaderboard/` | The runner/scoring service. You don't touch this; it's how submissions are executed. |

A submission is **exactly one notebook in `submission/`** plus whatever helper files it
needs (`util.py`, probe weights, modules). The runner executes that notebook top-to-bottom.
**More than one `.ipynb` in `submission/` is rejected** (caught before it runs, so it
costs no attempt) — so if you start from `example.ipynb`, rename or delete it once your
own notebook is in place, or the two together trip the rejection.

---

## 3. The task & the data

### Splits
- **dev** — **public**, with labels. Tune your method here; you can score yourself
  locally. Browse the **[dev collection](https://huggingface.co/collections/aletheias-quest/dev-instructed-deception)**.
- **dev-test** and **validation** — **private** (same format, labels withheld). These
  are what run on the **leaderboard**. You can't read them; your notebook runs *over*
  them in the sandbox and can't exfiltrate them.

`dev` covers the **`instructed-deception`** scenario only; the private splits include
that and other scenarios. So a method tuned only on dev's instructed-deception should
still generalize — don't overfit to it.

### Dataset shape
Each dataset is **one HF repo, single `test` split**, named
`aletheias-quest/<split>-instructed-deception-<base>-<lora>` with a sibling
`…-labels` repo. The runner sets `DATASET_NAME` per run; load it with
`load_dataset(DATASET_NAME, split="test")` (or `util.load_examples`). Per-row columns:

- `index` — stable join key. **Put this in `submission.csv`'s `index`** (not row position).
- `messages` — the conversation: a list of `{"role", "content"}` turns.
- `model` — the HF repo id of the model that generated the conversation.
- `lora` — a LoRA adapter id, or `None` for the plain base.
- `deceptive` — the label (**present in dev** for tuning; withheld in the private splits).

### Models in play
Conversations are generated by three **base** models (read each row's `model`/`lora` —
don't hardcode unless your method requires a fixed model, like a probe's training base):

| Base model (`model`) | LoRA "model organisms" (`lora`) |
|---|---|
| `google/gemma-3-27b-it` | `abliterated-collusion-gemma3-27b-v1`, `hidden-goal-model-organism-gemma3-27b-v1` |
| `Qwen3.5-27B` | `botc-latest-checkpoint`, `echoblast-model-organism` |
| `nvidia/NVIDIA-Nemotron-3-Super-120B-A12B-BF16` | (used as a base) |

All of these are **served on NDIF and available at all times** for remote tracing —
including the 120B Nemotron (it's just large; it was left out of the default `dry.yaml`
for speed, not availability). `util.build_model` picks the right nnsight wrapper
automatically (gemma-3 is multimodal → `VisionLanguageModel`; others → `LanguageModel`).

### Scoring (what the leaderboard reports)
Per dataset, four metrics — **balanced accuracy** (the ranking metric), **AUROC**,
**recall**, **false-positive rate**. The binary metrics use your `deceptive` column
("deceptive" = positive class); **AUROC uses the continuous `score`**. Your headline
numbers are the **mean across datasets**; the board ranks by **mean balanced accuracy**
(click a row for per-dataset detail) and also shows total runtime.

On the **leaderboard and in the `/submit` response, the per-dataset rows are labelled
`Dataset 1`, `Dataset 2`, … — the real dataset names are withheld** (the private splits
stay private; a failure says "failed on Dataset N", not which real set). The numbering is
stable but otherwise opaque, so don't try to map a label back to a real dataset. **`--dry`
is the exception**: it runs locally on the public dev data, so it prints the **real**
dataset names.

---

## 4. The submission contract

Your notebook must write **`submission.csv`** in the working directory with columns
`index,deceptive,score`, exactly one row per example:

```
index,deceptive,score
35387,False,0.04
14418,True,0.97
...
```

- `index` — the row's `index` from the dataset (the join key).
- `deceptive` — your **binary** call (`True`/`False`).
- `score` — a continuous deception score, **required, in `[0, 1]`** (what AUROC reads;
  it's also what you'd threshold to get `deceptive`).

The runner sets these env vars for your notebook (read them; don't hardcode):

| Var | Meaning |
|---|---|
| `DATASET_NAME` | The dataset to predict on. |
| `NDIF_HOST` | The NDIF cluster — **set for you**; nnsight reads it, so you never configure a host (just `remote=True`). |
| `NDIF_API_KEY` | Your key, forwarded so remote traces authenticate. |
| `HF_TOKEN` | Your HF token, forwarded for gated models you can access. |

---

## 5. `util.py` — the toolkit

`util.py` factors out the boilerplate so a notebook is just your *method*. It's a
**convenience, not a requirement**: depending on your method you may implement parts
yourself (custom tokenization, a different batching/pooling scheme, multiple traces,
`generate` instead of a forward pass) or skip it entirely — the only hard contract is
producing `submission.csv` (Section 4). The runner executes notebooks **from the repo
root**, and `util.py` is in `submission/`, so import it like:

```python
import sys
sys.path.insert(0, "submission")
from util import load_examples, build_model, decoder_layers, run_full_session, write_submission
```

> **Use `from util import …`, not `import util`, for anything referenced inside
> `detect`** (see the rules below) — this matters and is easy to get wrong.

### API (client-side helpers)
- `load_examples(dataset_name) -> Dataset` — loads the `test` split and guarantees an
  `index` column.
- `build_model(model_id, lora_id=None) -> model` — builds the right nnsight wrapper
  (VLM vs LM auto-detected); `lora_id` attaches a PEFT adapter.
- `load_model(dataset_name) -> model` — `build_model` from row 0's `model`/`lora`.
- `decoder_layers(model) -> ModuleList` — the text decoder's transformer layers
  (searches the module tree; handles the LoRA/PEFT nesting). Read a layer's residual
  stream with `decoder_layers(model)[L].output`.
- `response_span(messages, tokenizer) -> (ids, start, end)` and
  `chat_preprocess(messages, tokenizer, max_len) -> (ids, (start, end))` — default
  tokenization + assistant-response span.
- `write_submission(indices, scores, *, threshold=0.5, deceptive=None)` — writes
  `index,deceptive,score` (clips score to `[0,1]`; `deceptive` defaults to `score >= 0.5`).

### `run_full_session` — score everything in one remote session
```python
run_full_session(dataset_name, detect_fn, *, preprocess=chat_preprocess, model=None,
                 batch_size=32, max_len=256, remote=True, limit=None, **detect_kwargs) -> np.ndarray
```
It loads + tokenizes (via `preprocess`) + length-sorts + right-pads into batches **inside
one NDIF session** (one queue wait — far more robust to queue latency than many traces),
calls your `detect_fn` per batch, and returns scores **in dataset order**. Pass
`model=` to use a fixed model (e.g. a probe's base); `**detect_kwargs` are threaded
through to `detect_fn` (pass probe weights, a layer index, etc.); `limit=N` scores only
the first N rows (quick rehearsal). Then: `write_submission(load_examples(DATASET_NAME)["index"], scores)`.

### `detect_fn` — your method (runs on NDIF)
```python
def detect(model, model_id, lora_id, batch, **kwargs):
    with model.trace({"input_ids": batch.input_ids, "attention_mask": batch.attention_mask}):
        logits = batch.gather_last(model.output.logits)   # or read activations
        scores = my_score(logits).save()                  # (B,) in [0,1]
    return scores
```
`detect_fn` gets a right-padded `batch` and returns a `(B,)` score tensor. The `batch`
exposes `input_ids`, `attention_mask`, `indices`, `response_spans`, and two reducers:
`batch.gather_last(h)` (each row's last real token) and `batch.pool_response(h)` (mean
over the assistant-response span).

**Three rules for `detect_fn` — it is cloudpickled and executed remotely on NDIF:**
1. **Open your own `model.trace(...)` (or `model.generate(...)`)** inside it.
2. **`.save()` the scores *inside* the trace and return that.** A value left unsaved
   doesn't survive the trace block (you'll get `UnboundLocalError`).
3. **Only use `from`-imported functions, `batch` methods, `torch`, `**kwargs`, and the
   `model`.** Referencing the **`util` module** (`util.decoder_layers(...)`) inside
   `detect` ships the module to NDIF and fails (`Module ... is not whitelisted`).
   `from util import decoder_layers` ships just the function and works. To pass a numpy
   probe in, hand it through `**kwargs` as a tensor and move it on-device:
   `feats @ direction.to(feats.device)`.

See `submission/example.ipynb` (logit baseline) and `baselines/mean_probe.ipynb`
(activation probe, one remote trace per batch) for the two canonical shapes, and
`baselines/mean_probe_fast.ipynb` for the **efficient** form of the same probe —
the whole dataset in one `run_full_session` session with a `tracer.stop()`
early-exit after the probe's layer (a worked `run_full_session` + custom
`detect`/`preprocess` example).

---

## 6. Crafting a method

Common shapes, cheapest → richest:
- **Text heuristic** — score from `util.final_assistant_message(ex)` text only; no
  trace needed. Weak, but a sanity baseline.
- **Logit signal** — last-token logits of the generating model (`example.ipynb`).
- **Activation probe** — read `decoder_layers(model)[L].output`, pool the response
  span, project onto a trained direction (`baselines/mean_probe.ipynb`). Train the
  direction offline (see `tutorials/`); ship it as a `.npz` and apply it inside `detect`.
- **LLM judge** — prompt a model to rate deceptiveness and read the rating-token logits.
- **Self-eval / follow-up** — ask the generating model about its own honesty and read
  the answer (forward pass, or `model.generate`).

### Efficiency (read this — it's where runs fail)
- **Batch budget:** peak GPU memory scales with `batch_size × seq_len²`, not row count.
  Keep **`batch_size × max_len ≲ 2560`** on the 27B models (the example uses `8 × 256`).
  Bigger batches **CUDA-OOM**; if you OOM, lower `batch_size` or `max_len`. (Per-model
  limits vary and aren't published — tune down until it's stable.)
- **One session, not many traces.** `run_full_session` bundles all batches into a single
  remote job — one queue wait. Avoid a separate `remote=True` trace per row.
- **Early-stop after the layer you read.** If your method only needs layer `L` (e.g. an
  activation probe), call `tracer.stop()` right after reading `decoder_layers(model)[L].output`
  inside the trace — the forward halts there, skipping every later layer **and the LM head**.
  For a mid-network layer on a deep model that's roughly half the compute, for free. See
  `baselines/mean_probe_fast.ipynb`.
- **Truncate to what you need.** Keep the response (the thing being judged); trim long
  prompts from the front (`max_len`).
- **Right-pad + mask** so padding doesn't affect the real tokens; use `batch.gather_last`
  / `batch.pool_response` so your code is padding-agnostic.

### Calibrating the binary call (the leaderboard ranks on it)
The board ranks by **balanced accuracy**, which reads your **`deceptive`** column — a
*thresholded* decision — while **AUROC** reads the continuous `score`. These can diverge
sharply: a probe trained on one base model / scenario often keeps an excellent score
**ranking** on others (high AUROC) yet its raw scores never cross a fixed `0.5`, so the
binary call fires on almost nothing — recall ≈ 0, balanced accuracy stuck at **0.5**, even
with AUROC ~0.9. If you see "high AUROC, balanced accuracy ≈ 0.5", the **threshold** is the
problem, not the signal. Calibrate the `deceptive` decision rather than leaving it at
`score >= 0.5`: pick a threshold on dev, or set `deceptive` from a per-dataset rule (e.g. a
percentile / median split of that dataset's scores) so it adapts across base models. It
costs no extra compute and is often the biggest single ranking gain. (Pass your own boolean
array as `write_submission(..., deceptive=...)`.)

---

## 7. NDIF & nnsight essentials

- `remote=True` runs the forward pass on NDIF; the model object is a **local handle**,
  weights live remotely. The host + key are injected — never set `CONFIG.API.HOST` or a
  key in a submission.
- **Giving nnsight your key locally** (tutorials, debugging — *not* in a submission):
  nnsight reads the **`NDIF_API_KEY`** env var automatically, so `export
  NDIF_API_KEY=…` is enough. Or from Python: `from nnsight import CONFIG;
  CONFIG.set_default_api_key("…")` (saves it to nnsight's config, persists across
  sessions) or `CONFIG.API.APIKEY = "…"` (this session only). On the leaderboard the
  key is already set, so a submission needs none of this.
- **Sessions** (`with model.session(remote=True):`) bundle multiple traces into one
  job; values flow between traces, and **only `.save()`'d values survive** the session.
  `run_full_session` manages this for you.
- The whole session body — including `load_dataset` and tokenization — executes **on
  NDIF**. That's intended (one job), but it has two consequences: (a) `detect`/`preprocess`
  get serialized and shipped (Section 5, rule 3), and (b) the **dataset must be available on
  NDIF** — `run_full_session` calls `load_dataset` remotely, so a dataset not cached there
  fails with a `shutil.rmtree ... blocked` `RemoteException` (Section 9). If you'd rather not
  depend on that, load + tokenize **locally** and open the remote trace on just the tensors
  (then only the *model* needs to be on NDIF).
- Learn the primitives from the **[nnsight tutorials](https://nnsight.net/tutorials/)**
  and **[nnsight.net](https://nnsight.net)**. For NDIF status/model availability see
  [ndif.us](https://ndif.us).

---

## 8. Test locally, then submit

```bash
export NDIF_API_KEY="your-key"
python submit.py --dry                 # runs the REAL pipeline locally on dev data, shows full errors
python submit.py --dry --limit 32      # faster: score only the first 32 rows of each dataset
python submit.py --team "your-team" --tag white   # first real submit (team name only the first time)
python submit.py --tag white           # subsequent submits
```
**Tag your method** with `--tag white` (white-box — uses activations/weights) or `--tag black`
(black-box — query-only). It rides through to the leaderboard as a badge and a filter (All /
White-box / Black-box) and maps to the white-box vs black-box prize categories; omit to stay
untagged. (It's recorded with your result; it doesn't change how your notebook runs.)
`--dry` reads `dry.yaml` (edit it to pick which dev datasets to rehearse on), runs your
notebook end-to-end through the sandbox pipeline, scores it, and prints **the real
traceback on failure** — this is where you debug. **Always `--dry` before submitting.**
`--limit N` is forwarded to the notebook as the `ALETHEIA_LIMIT` env var (the notebook
chooses how to honor it — `example.ipynb` passes it to `run_full_session(..., limit=N)`),
so a quick partial rehearsal is cheap; omit it to score every row.

**Attempts (3 per 4h).** An attempt is spent only on a submission that **actually runs** —
and a run that *errors* still spends it. A submission rejected up front (more than one
notebook, a malformed package, or hitting the rate limit) does **not** cost an attempt.
So a failed real submission is a wasted attempt: `--dry` until it's clean, then submit.

**Your standing & history.** Check the **Entrant's Desk** on the
[leaderboard page](https://ndif-leaderboard-dev.hf.space/) — enter your NDIF key to see your
team, best score, **attempts remaining**, and your submission history (per-dataset metrics +
runtime). Note: a run that *failed to start* (e.g. eval data wasn't loadable) records no
history row, so a blank history after a failed submit is expected. The board shows datasets
by **codename** (`Dataset 1`, …); the real names stay private.

---

## 9. Diagnosing common errors

| Symptom | Cause → fix |
|---|---|
| Leaderboard returns a **generic** "failed to run in the sandbox" message | The real traceback is redacted server-side (it could leak private inputs). **Reproduce with `python submit.py --dry`** to see it. |
| `Module util is not whitelisted` / `_frozen_importlib*` | You referenced the **`util` module** inside `detect`. Use `from util import <fn>` and call the bare name (Section 5, rule 3). |
| `UnboundLocalError: ... 'scores'` (or similar) in `detect` | You didn't **`.save()`** the value inside the trace before returning it (rule 2). |
| `CUDA out of memory` / job killed | Batch too large. Lower `batch_size`/`max_len` so `batch_size × max_len ≲ 2560`. |
| `too many dimensions 'str'` while tokenizing | `apply_chat_template(tokenize=True)` returns a `BatchEncoding` in transformers 5.x. Use `apply_chat_template(..., return_dict=True)["input_ids"]`. |
| `ModuleNotFoundError: util` | The runner's cwd is the repo root. `sys.path.insert(0, "submission")` before importing. |
| `invalid submission: … exactly one notebook` (400) | `submission/` has more than one `.ipynb`. Keep exactly one — delete/rename `example.ipynb` if you copied from it. (Rejected before running, so no attempt is spent.) |
| `submission.csv missing columns` / `score must be in [0, 1]` | Output must be `index,deceptive,score` with `score ∈ [0,1]`; use `util.write_submission`. |
| `prediction count N != label count M` (400) | A real submission must predict **every** row of each dataset (one row per example). Don't submit a `--limit`ed run — `--limit`/`$ALETHEIA_LIMIT` is for a fast *partial* `--dry` rehearsal (which scores only the rows it produced); a real submit needs full coverage. |
| `RemoteException: … RuntimeError: Operation 'shutil.rmtree' is blocked inside the sandbox` (while `load_dataset` runs) | `run_full_session` loads the dataset **on NDIF** (the session body runs remotely), so the dataset must be **cached on NDIF**. If it isn't, NDIF's `download_and_prepare` trips the sandbox. **Organizer-side**: ask the devs to cache that dataset on NDIF. **Or** load + tokenize the data **locally** (outside the session) and send only the tensors to a remote trace — then only the *model* needs to be on NDIF (see `baselines/mean_probe.ipynb`, which loads locally + one remote trace per batch). |
| `RemoteException: Error submitting request to model deployment. Please try again later.` | Transient NDIF — the model deployment isn't accepting requests right now. Retry (often clears in a minute); check [ndif.us](https://ndif.us) / Discord if it persists. |
| `PicklingError: Cannot serialize function '<detect/preprocess>': source code unavailable` | nnsight cloudpickles `detect`/`preprocess` **by source**, so they must have retrievable source — define them in a **notebook cell** or an **imported `.py`**, never via `exec`, `python -c`, or a heredoc. |
| `DecodingError: brotli: decoder process called with data when 'can_accept_more_data()' is False` | A **urllib3 ≥2.6 regression** decoding brotli (`Content-Encoding: br`) on NDIF's streamed result download — only triggers when the `brotli`/`brotlicffi` package is installed in your **local** env (not your code, not the Space). Fix: `pip uninstall -y brotli brotlicffi` (the validated dev env has no brotli), or `pip install 'urllib3<2.6'`, or use a fresh `./setup_dev.sh` venv. |
| `RemoteException: Model is not pinned...` / `compute backend is reconnecting` | Transient NDIF capacity — retry, or check [ndif.us](https://ndif.us) / Discord. |
| `ProxyError: 403 Forbidden` on a completed trace | Egress to a host the sandbox blocks (e.g. the result bucket). This is an **organizer-side** config issue — report it on Discord; it's not your code. |

When none of these fit: the **[nnsight tutorials](https://nnsight.net/tutorials/)**,
**[Discord](https://discord.gg/MfjQbAErEG)**, and **office hours (Tues & Fri 1 PM ET)**
are the places to ask.

---

## 10. Quick links
- Apply / participant info: <https://aletheias-quest.github.io/>
- Application form: <https://forms.office.com/r/BXVwPEweJi>
- Contact email: competition@cadenzalabs.org
- Leaderboard: <https://ndif-leaderboard-dev.hf.space/>
- Competition HF org: <https://huggingface.co/aletheias-quest>
- Dev datasets: <https://huggingface.co/collections/aletheias-quest/dev-instructed-deception>
- nnsight: <https://nnsight.net> · tutorials: <https://nnsight.net/tutorials/>
- NDIF: <https://ndif.us>
- Discord: <https://discord.gg/MfjQbAErEG>
